construct_description: The ability to investigate a mid-year budget overrun, identify root causes with quantified impact, spot data inconsistencies, and communicate a credible corrective action recommendation to a CFO — a core competency for finance analysts, department controllers, and healthcare finance professionals.

pba_category.name: AI-Augmented Performance Tasks
pba_category.description: Tasks where the test-taker uses AI tools as part of their workflow. Assessment focuses on how well they leverage and evaluate AI, not just the final output.

skills.name: Financial Analysis
skills.description: Interpreting budget vs. actuals data, identifying root causes, and quantifying variance accurately — including spotting data inconsistencies.

skills.name: Communication
skills.description: Structuring a clear, credible brief for a CFO that explains the variance and recommends a corrective path with supporting evidence.

skills.name: Critical Evaluation
skills.description: Identifying a data inconsistency between two source documents, evaluating AI recommendations against clinical realities, and flagging unsupported conclusions.

skills.name: Risk Assessment
skills.description: Recognizing downstream risks of corrective options — including clinical impact of deferring capital projects.

scenario.title: The Budget Variance Brief
scenario.briefing:
The Clinical Technology department at Northgate Health System is $218,000 over budget with 5 months remaining in the fiscal year. The CFO, Dr. Patricia Lam, is presenting to the board next Tuesday and needs a brief that clearly explains what happened and recommends a corrective action path.

Three candidate root causes have been surfaced by the department head: (1) an unplanned expansion of a vendor contract renewal that was processed without a budget amendment, (2) two emergency software licenses purchased outside the capital plan, and (3) a contractor rate increase mid-year that was not reflected in the original headcount budget.

Three corrective options are on the table: reallocate budget from the Administrative Technology reserve, request a supplemental budget from the CFO reserve fund, or defer two capital projects to the next fiscal year.

Your job is to write a one-page brief for Dr. Lam before the board meeting. The AI Workbench has been loaded with the budget data and supporting documents. Review the materials carefully — there is a numeric discrepancy between two documents that needs to be flagged.

inbox.subject: Clinical Tech variance — need brief before board meeting
inbox.body:
I need a one-pager before Tuesday's board meeting.

Clinical Tech is $218K over budget with 5 months left in the year. I need to walk the board through: (1) what caused this — real root causes, not 'unexpected costs', (2) how much each cause contributed, and (3) what we're going to do about it.

I've looked at three corrective options — reallocation from Admin Tech reserve, supplemental budget request, or capital project deferral. I need a recommendation, not just a list of options. If you recommend deferral, you need to tell me which projects and flag any downstream risk.

One more thing: my team thinks there's a number that doesn't add up between the actuals report and the vendor contract email. Find it and flag it. I don't want to quote a wrong number in front of the board.

— Dr. Patricia Lam
CFO, Northgate Health System

inbox.subject: RE: Clinical Tech Budget — department head context
inbox.body:
Dr. Lam asked me to give you background.

Here's my read on what happened:

1. The Medivance EHR support contract renewal: our vendor proposed an expanded scope (added 24/7 on-call coverage, we needed it after the March outage) and the renewal was processed at the new rate before finance got the budget amendment paperwork in. The difference is somewhere between $89K and $94K depending on which number you believe — there's a discrepancy I can't explain.

2. Emergency software licenses: we had two situations. First, a ransomware incident in April forced us to purchase Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR) software on an emergency basis for $41,200. Second, we brought in a temporary telehealth overflow platform for 60 days during the staffing shortage ($28,600). Neither was in the capital plan.

3. Contractor rate increase: we have 3 IT contractors whose hourly rate went from $95 to $118/hr effective March 1st, per their contract escalation clause. Our budget was built on the old rate. The overrun on that line for the year (full year impact) will be $54,000 — with roughly $36,750 of that in the rear-view already.

For the corrective path: I want to flag that one of the two capital projects on the deferral list is the medical imaging archive migration. Deferring that has real clinical risk — we're still storing legacy DICOM files on a system past end-of-life. The other project (facilities management software upgrade) has no patient safety implications.

— Kevin Strauss
Director, Clinical Technology, Northgate Health System

sources.title: Medivance EHR Support — Contract Renewal Email Chain
sources.author: Kevin Strauss / Medivance Account Team
sources.publication: Internal email archive — March of this fiscal year
sources.content_html:
Medivance EHR Support Contract Renewal — Email Chain

From: Jordan Park, Account Executive <j.park@medivance.example>
To: Kevin Strauss <k.strauss@northgatehealth.example>
Date: March 4
Subject: Contract Renewal — Expanded Support Scope Proposal

Kevin,

Per our conversation last week, here is the formal proposal for expanding your support tier from Standard to Premium Plus, which includes 24/7 on-call engineering coverage, a dedicated account manager, and quarterly system health reviews.

Standard annual support (current): $312,000/year
Premium Plus annual support (proposed): $401,000/year
Incremental annual increase: $89,000

This price is locked for a 2-year renewal term. Happy to schedule a call to walk through the added services.

— Jordan Park, Medivance

From: Kevin Strauss <k.strauss@northgatehealth.example>
To: Jordan Park
Date: March 6
Subject: RE: Contract Renewal

Jordan — we're proceeding with Premium Plus. I'll get the PO to you by end of week. I've flagged to our finance team that the annual cost is going up by $89K. They'll process a budget amendment.

— Kevin

From: Jordan Park
To: Kevin Strauss
Date: March 8
Subject: RE: RE: Contract Renewal — Signed Agreement Attached

Kevin — great, signed agreement attached. Note that billing cycles to the new rate effective April 1. Looking forward to the expanded partnership.

— Jordan

[Attachment: Medivance_NorthgateHS_PremiumPlus_Renewal_Signed.pdf]

sources.title: IT Contractor Rate Escalation — Formal Notice
sources.author: TechForce Staffing Solutions
sources.content_html:
TechForce Staffing Solutions — Rate Escalation Notice

To: Northgate Health System — Clinical Technology
Re: Annual Rate Adjustment per Contract Section 8.3
Effective: March 1 of current fiscal year

Pursuant to Section 8.3 of the Master Staffing Agreement (annual CPI-based rate adjustment), TechForce is implementing the following rate change for all active IT contractors placed at Northgate Health System:

ContractorRolePrior RateNew RateAdjustment
Marcus ObiSr. Systems Analyst$95/hr$118/hr+$23/hr (+24.2%)
Fatima Al-RashidNetwork Infrastructure Specialist$95/hr$118/hr+$23/hr (+24.2%)
David ParkEHR Integration Analyst$95/hr$118/hr+$23/hr (+24.2%)

Basis for adjustment: 2024 CPI-W (All Urban Wage Earners) = 4.1%. Contract Section 8.3 allows adjustment up to CPI + 20 percentage points at TechForce's discretion. This adjustment reflects current market rates for specialized healthcare IT contractors.

Assumed hours per contractor (FY budget basis): 2,000 hours/year (full-time equivalent)

Incremental cost per contractor per year: $23 × 2,000 hrs = $46,000

Combined incremental cost (3 contractors): $138,000/year — however, effective for only 10 months of the current FY (March through December): $115,000 FY impact

Note: The rate adjustment notice was received on February 22. Northgate's budget for the current year was finalized on December 15 of the prior year and did not include this rate change.

sources.title: Clinical Technology — FY Capital Project Schedule
sources.author: Northgate Health System Finance / Clinical Technology
sources.publication: Approved Capital Plan, current fiscal year
sources.content_html:
Clinical Technology — Approved Capital Projects (Current FY)

ProjectBudgetStatusCompletion TargetClinical Risk if Deferred
Medical Imaging Archive Migration (DICOM)$187,000Planning phase — not yet startedQ4 this FYHIGH — legacy storage system is past end-of-life; vendor support expired 8 months ago. System failure would make archived imaging studies inaccessible. HIPAA audit risk.
Facilities Management Software Upgrade (CMMS)$94,000Vendor selected — contract not yet signedQ4 this FYLOW — current CMMS is functional; upgrade adds preventive maintenance scheduling and compliance reporting features. No patient safety dependency.
Nurse Call System Expansion (Floor 4 West)$62,000Installation 70% completeQ3 this FY (imminent)HIGH — cannot be deferred; construction already underway. Partial installation would be a safety hazard.

Admin Technology Reserve (Separate Budget)

The Administrative Technology department carries an unencumbered reserve of $310,000 designated for "technology infrastructure contingencies." Use of this reserve requires CFO approval and a documented business case. The reserve has not been drawn against in the past 3 years.

Note: Deferring only the CMMS upgrade would free up $94,000. Deferring only the DICOM migration would free up $187,000. Deferring both would free up $281,000 — which, combined with favorable variances in other lines (~$20K), would approximately close the projected year-end gap of $236K.

ai_workbench.persona: Healthcare Finance AI Assistant — has been loaded with the budget vs. actuals report, vendor emails, contractor rate notice, and capital project schedule
ai_workbench.known_limitation: The AI correctly identifies the three root causes but recommends deferring capital projects as the primary corrective path without weighing the clinical risk of deferring the DICOM imaging archive migration. It also does not flag the numeric discrepancy between the vendor contract email and the actuals report.
ai_workbench.seed_response:
Here's my analysis of the Northgate Clinical Tech budget variance:

**Root Cause Summary:**
1. Medivance contract expansion: ~$94K unfavorable (per the actuals report)
2. Emergency software licenses: $69,800 unfavorable (EDR $41,200 + telehealth platform $28,600)
3. Contractor rate increase: $54,000 projected year-end impact ($36,750 incurred YTD)

**Total root cause accounted: ~$217,800** — consistent with the $218,550 YTD variance.

**Recommended corrective path:** Deferring the two remaining capital projects (DICOM migration and CMMS upgrade) would free up $281,000 — more than enough to offset the projected year-end overrun of $236K with a small buffer. This approach avoids a supplemental budget request (which requires board approval) and preserves the Admin Tech reserve for true emergencies.

**My recommendation:** Defer both capital projects and apply the freed budget against the operating overrun. This is the cleanest accounting solution and avoids escalating to the board for additional funds.

rubric.root_cause_accuracy: Correctly identifies all three root causes with quantified amounts — vendor contract ~$89-94K, emergency licenses $69,800, contractor rate increase $54K (projected) / $36,750 (YTD)
rubric.data_inconsistency_flagged: Identifies and flags the discrepancy between the actuals report ($94,000 vendor variance) and the vendor contract email chain ($89,000 incremental cost per Jordan Park's proposal and Kevin's confirmation) — the $5,000 difference needs to be reconciled before the CFO quotes a number to the board
rubric.clinical_risk_acknowledged: Does not recommend deferring the DICOM imaging archive migration without explicitly flagging the clinical and HIPAA risk — the AI's recommendation to defer both projects omits this critical caveat
rubric.recommendation_specificity: Makes a clear corrective path recommendation with specific dollar amounts and source — not a generic 'explore options'
rubric.contractor_math: Correctly notes that the contractor rate increase's full-year impact is different from the actuals report number — the rate change was effective March 1, meaning 10 months of impact in this FY, and the actuals report shows only 7/12 of that
